properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Logan Square Blue Line Station
properties.description: 1895 Chicago Elevated station. Arts & Crafts brick shelter house. Gateway to Logan Square and Avondale neighborhoods.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Milwaukee Avenue Corridor
properties.description: Historic commercial spine of Avondale. Working-class storefronts, Polish delis, and newer Latino businesses reflect successive immigrant waves since the 1880s.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: St. Wenceslaus Roman Catholic Church
properties.description: Czech congregation founded 1863. Romanesque Revival sanctuary, 1904. Anchored Czech immigrant community; now serves a predominantly Latino congregation.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Avondale Club
properties.description: 1920s neighborhood social hall. Hosted Polish fraternal organizations, dances, and union meetings through the mid-twentieth century.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Peoples Gas Light & Coke Company Substation
properties.description: Industrial-era utility substation. Brick Romanesque Revival facade. Recalls Avondale's transition from residential to mixed industrial use in early 1900s.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Polish Triangle / Copernicus Plaza
properties.description: Major Polish community landmark. Statue of astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus installed 1973. Anchor of Polish commercial and cultural life on Milwaukee Ave.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Northwest Tower (Milshire Hotel)
properties.description: Art Deco skyscraper, 1929, by Perkins, Chatten & Hammond. One of Chicago's earliest moderne towers; prominent gateway structure on the Milwaukee Ave corridor.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Kurowski Sausage Shop & Rich's Bakery
properties.description: Established 1997. Carries on the tradition of the original Kurowski Sausage (est. 1945, closed early 2000s). Traditional Polish foods — sausages, baked goods, paczki — in a mixed-use storefront.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: St. Hyacinth Basilica
properties.description: Polish Cathedral, 1917–1921. Renaissance/Baroque Revival by Worthman and Steinach. Three-towered facade, 3000 sq ft copper dome, 175+ relics, ornate murals. Now offers services in English, Polish, and Spanish.

properties.author: Aurian Azghandi
properties.name: Kosciuszko Park Fieldhouse
properties.description: Tudor Revival fieldhouse, 1916, by Schwartz and Bowes. 9.01 acres. Added to National Register of Historic Places October 16, 2013. Named after Polish hero Thadeuz Kosciuszko.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Fulton Market Cold Storage
properties.description: 1920s industrial cold storage building. Brick and concrete construction. Former hub of Chicago's wholesale meatpacking and produce distribution. Now converted to office and retail.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Randolph Street Market District
properties.description: Historic wholesale produce and restaurant supply corridor. Brick warehouse facades from 1890s–1930s. Now anchors the restaurant row and nightlife district.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: The Warehouse (Soho House)
properties.description: Former industrial warehouse converted to members club. Exposed brick, timber framing, and industrial windows retained. Emblematic of the neighborhood's adaptive reuse wave.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Fulton Market Mural Wall
properties.address: N Peoria St & W Lake St
properties.description: Series of large-scale murals commissioned during the neighborhood's revitalization period. Documents the tension between industrial heritage and creative-class gentrification.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: McDonald's Global HQ
properties.description: Corporate campus relocated from Oak Brook, opened 2018. Glass and steel contemporary design by Ross Barney Architects. Anchor of the tech/corporate transformation of the district.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Haymarket Square Memorial
properties.description: Site of the 1886 Haymarket affair and labor movement flashpoint. Police monument removed 1970; worker memorial installed 2004. One of the most significant labor history sites in the United States.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Warehouse Row Buildings
properties.description: Row of intact c.1900–1915 brick wholesale market buildings. Corbelled cornices, segmental arch windows, iron loading docks. Represent the pre-gentrification streetscape of the district.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: City Winery Chicago
properties.description: Adaptive reuse of industrial warehouse into winery, restaurant, and concert venue. Interior retains structural timber and brick. Example of cultural reuse of industrial fabric.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Skinner Park
properties.description: CPS fieldhouse and park in the southern transitional zone. Serves West Loop / Near West Side communities displaced by UIC Circle interchange and urban renewal.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: Greek Town Gateway
properties.address: S Halsted St & W Adams St
properties.description: Northern edge of the historic Greek American commercial corridor. Iron gateway arch, 1990s. Anchors Greek restaurant row, which has contracted significantly since the 1990s.

properties.author: Elif Koyuncul
properties.name: UIC Circle Interchange / Jane Addams Memorial
properties.description: Largest highway interchange in Illinois; its 1960s construction displaced thousands of Near West Side residents. Jane Addams Memorial (2012) marks the nearby site of Hull House.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: 63rd Street & Cottage Grove CTA Station
properties.description: Former terminus of the Jackson Park elevated line. Station dates to 1892; Chicago Landmark. The elevated structure shaped commercial and residential development along 63rd Street for decades.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Woodlawn Tap (Jimmy's)
properties.description: Working-class tavern opened 1948 by James Wilson. Known as "Jimmy's." A gathering place for University of Chicago students, South Side intellectuals, and neighborhood residents across racial lines.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Blackstone Avenue Historic District
properties.description: Late 19th-century row houses and greystone two-flats built for Hyde Park professionals. Intact streetscape; listed on National Register of Historic Places.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Woodlawn African Methodist Episcopal Church
properties.description: Founded 1872. One of the oldest Black congregations on the South Side. Sanctuary dates to 1914. Site of community organizing during the Civil Rights era and Great Migration.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Site of the Regal Theater
properties.description: Demolished 1973. Once Chicago's premier venue for Black performers — B.B. King, Aretha Franklin, Sam Cooke all performed here. Its loss remains a touchstone of Bronzeville and South Side cultural memory.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Obama Presidential Center (under construction)
properties.description: Site of the planned Obama Presidential Center in Jackson Park. Contested through litigation over historic landscape impacts; construction began 2021. Intersection of preservation, equity, and civic legacy.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Midway Plaisance
properties.description: Frederick Law Olmsted-designed parkway connecting Washington and Jackson Parks. Site of the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition midway. Chicago Landmark and contributing element to the Jackson Park National Historic Landmark.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: Hyde Park–Kenwood Urban Renewal Corridor
properties.description: Block-by-block evidence of 1950s–1970s URA clearance programs that removed much of Woodlawn's housing stock. Vacant lots and superblocks trace the boundaries of federally funded demolition.

properties.author: Pete Smith
properties.name: University of Chicago Smart Museum
properties.description: Contemporary museum on the U of C campus. Sited adjacent to former Woodlawn residential blocks demolished during urban renewal. Raises ongoing questions about institutional expansion and community displacement.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Victory Monument
properties.address: 35th St & S Martin Luther King Dr
properties.description: Bronze monument, 1927-1936, by Leonard Crunelle. Commemorates Black soldiers of the 8th Regiment, Illinois National Guard who served in World War I. Anchor of Bronzeville's civic landscape.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Chicago Defender Building
properties.description: Former home of the Chicago Defender, founded 1905. The Defender was instrumental in encouraging Black Southern migration north and became one of the most influential Black newspapers in America.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Ida B. Wells Homes (site)
properties.description: Site of demolished Ida B. Wells public housing project, built 1941, demolished 2011. Once housed thousands of Black families; its erasure raises unresolved questions about community memory and replacement development.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Eighth Regiment Armory
properties.description: Romanesque Revival armory, 1914. Home of the all-Black 8th Illinois Infantry. One of the most architecturally distinguished armories in the Midwest; Chicago Landmark.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Blues Heaven Foundation (Chess Records)
properties.description: Site of Chess Records, founded 1950. Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, Howlin' Wolf, and Etta James all recorded here. Now the Blues Heaven Foundation museum; National Historic Landmark.

properties.author: Davalos
properties.name: Wabash Avenue YMCA
properties.description: Built 1913. One of the first Black YMCAs in the nation. Provided housing, jobs training, and organizing space for Black Chicagoans during the Great Migration. Chicago Landmark.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Unity Hall
properties.description: Built 1886; Chicago Landmark. Originally a business block, later used as headquarters of the Chicago Black Renaissance and site of political organizing. One of the most historically dense buildings in Bronzeville.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: St. James Catholic Church
properties.description: Gothic Revival, 1875. Served successive immigrant communities — Irish, German, Italian — before becoming a historically Black parish. Intact stone tower and stained glass windows.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Calumet-Giles-Prairie District
properties.description: National Register Historic District. Row houses and two-flats built 1880s–1910s for working and middle-class residents. One of the better-preserved Victorian residential blocks on the South Side.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Giles Avenue Greystone Row
properties.description: Intact row of ca. 1890 greystone two-flats and three-flats. Represents the standard working-class housing typology of the Near South Side before speculative demolition and disinvestment.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Mercy Hospital (original building)
properties.description: Founded 1852 by the Sisters of Mercy. Original Victorian-era building altered over time. Oldest continuously operating hospital in Illinois; served Black patients when other hospitals maintained segregated facilities.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Former Illinois National Guard Armory
properties.description: Large brick armory complex, early twentieth century. Reflects the role of Illinois National Guard units in South Side community life, including the all-Black 8th Regiment.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Douglas Park
properties.description: William Le Baron Jenney–designed park, 1871. Named after Stephen A. Douglas. Part of Chicago's "emerald necklace" of West Side parks. Formally designed landscape with lagoon and boathouse.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Stephen A. Douglas Tomb
properties.description: Monument to U.S. Senator Stephen Douglas, erected 1881. Bronze figure atop a 96-foot column. Site of Douglas's country estate, "Oakenwald." Illinois State Memorial.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Comiskey Park Site / Guaranteed Rate Field
properties.description: Site of original Comiskey Park (1910–1990) and its successor stadium (1991–present). Home of the Chicago White Sox. Demolition of old Comiskey raised major preservation debates about mid-century ballparks.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Dearborn Homes
properties.description: Chicago Housing Authority project, 1950. Low-rise brick construction by Loebl, Schlossman & Demuth. One of the smaller CHA developments; still standing and occupied, unlike many demolished South Side projects.

properties.tour_name: Douglas: Architecture, Art, History, and Disinvestment
properties.author: Gonsky
properties.name: Hammer and Palmer Mansion
properties.description: Italianate mansion, ca. 1880s. One building preserved for its architectural distinction; another for the activist work carried out within it. Reflects the uneven logic of individual landmark preservation in a landscape of broader disinvestment.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Union Stock Yard Gate
properties.address: W Exchange Ave & S Peoria St
properties.description: Triple-arched Lemont limestone gate, ca. 1879, attributed to Burnham & Root. The only surviving substantial structure from the 475-acre industrial complex. Chicago Landmark 1972; National Historic Landmark 1981.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: International Amphitheatre (site)
properties.description: Built 1934; demolished 1999. Seating 10,000+, it hosted livestock shows, boxing, concerts, and the 1968 Democratic National Convention. Its absence underscores how political and cultural memory outlasts physical structure.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: The Industrial Core / South Packers Avenue
properties.description: Heart of Packingtown. Long blocks, wide rights-of-way, and oversized parcels are remnants of a built environment engineered for rail sidings and industrial circulation. Swift pioneered the refrigerated railcar; Armour expanded vertically integrated factories. By 1900, the Yards supplied over half the nation's meat.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Labor Conditions and Upton Sinclair's Chicago
properties.description: Slaughterhouses were among the most hazardous workplaces in the nation. Upton Sinclair's observations here, published in The Jungle (1906), catalyzed the Pure Food and Drug Act and Meat Inspection Act. The social geography — long blocks, worker cottages, housing adjacent to industry — remains legible.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Bubbly Creek (Canal Origins Park)
properties.description: South Fork of the Chicago River. Stockyards operations discharged blood, offal, manure, and chemical by-products directly into this channel for decades. The creek remains heavily polluted. One of the earliest and most visible examples of environmental injustice in American industrial history.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Worker Housing and Boarding Houses
properties.description: Modest boarding houses — dormered roofs, shallow gables, thin brick veneers — stood feet from industrial buildings. Ethnic succession shaped these blocks from Irish and German families to Polish, Lithuanian, Czech, Slovak, and later Mexican immigrants and African American laborers.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Stanley's Tavern (Whiskey Row site)
properties.description: Established 1924 by Polish immigrant Stanley Kurek. One of the last remnants of "Whiskey Row," a dense strip of worker bars at the edge of the Yards. Taverns served as places to cash paychecks, share news, and organize politically. Social infrastructure for a community facing economic precarity.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Stock Yards National Bank
properties.description: Colonial Revival, 1925, by Abraham Epstein. Modeled after Philadelphia's Independence Hall. At its peak, one of the largest banks outside downtown Chicago. Withstood the 1934 fire intact. Chicago Landmark 2008. Active $80 million proposal for adaptive reuse as music-recording complex with community spaces.

properties.tour_name: Vestiges and Traces of the "Hog Butcher for the World": Labor, Landscape, and Memory in the Union Stock Yards
properties.author: Yasmine Baldo
properties.name: Stockyards Industrial Park
properties.description: Post-industrial corridor occupying much of the former meatpacking district. Warehouses and trucking firms replaced killing floors and smokehouses. The name evokes industrial nostalgia while softening histories of pollution, labor exploitation, and community displacement. The oversized parcels and wide rail alignments are decisions made more than a century ago, still structuring space today.
